skill.name: Firmware Analyst
skill.description: Expert firmware analyst specializing in embedded systems, IoT security, and hardware reverse engineering. Provides comprehensive guidance for firmware extraction, analysis, and vulnerability research across routers, IoT devices, automotive systems, and industrial controllers.
skill.summary: Expert firmware analyst for embedded systems security analysis and vulnerability research
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This is a prompt-only skill containing only documentation in SKILL.md with no executable code. The skill provides educational content for firmware security analysis including extraction methods, analysis workflows, vulnerability classes, and ethical guidelines. Static analysis scanned 0 files and detected 0 issues. The skill includes clear ethical boundaries specifying appropriate use (authorized security audits, bug bounty, academic research) versus prohibited activities (unauthorized access, creating malicious firmware).

content.value_statement: This skill helps security professionals extract, analyze, and identify vulnerabilities in embedded firmware for routers, IoT devices, and industrial control systems through systematic analysis workflows.
actual_capabilities: Guide firmware extraction from devices via vendor downloads, UART, JTAG, or flash memory dumping
Provide systematic firmware analysis workflow covering identification, extraction, filesystem analysis, and binary analysis
Identify common vulnerability classes including hardcoded credentials, command injection, memory corruption, and information disclosure
Recommend appropriate tools for firmware analysis including binwalk, Ghidra, QEMU emulation, and hardware interfaces
Assist with setting up firmware emulation using QEMU and Firmadyne for dynamic analysis
Support creation of security assessment reports with findings, severity ratings, and remediation guidance
limitations: Cannot execute or analyze actual firmware files directly - provides guidance only
Does not have access to physical hardware interfaces or device access
Cannot guarantee vulnerability detection - findings require manual verification
Does not provide exploit code or attack payloads

use_cases.title: IoT Security Audit
use_cases.description: Conduct security assessment of a smart home device firmware to identify vulnerabilities before deployment

use_cases.title: Embedded Systems Research
use_cases.description: Analyze firmware from industrial control systems to understand architecture and identify security weaknesses

use_cases.title: Bug Bounty Investigation
use_cases.description: Investigate firmware vulnerabilities in consumer devices as part of authorized bug bounty programs
use_cases.target_user: Bug bounty hunters and ethical hackers

prompt_templates.title: Getting Started with Firmware Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me understand the firmware analysis process. I have a firmware binary and need to identify its architecture, extract the filesystem, and find potential security issues. What are the key steps?
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner exploring firmware analysis for the first time

prompt_templates.title: Extracting Firmware from Device
prompt_templates.prompt: What methods are available for extracting firmware from an IoT device? I need to analyze a smart router and want to understand the options from software extraction to hardware methods like UART.
prompt_templates.scenario: Analyst needing to acquire firmware for analysis

prompt_templates.title: Identifying Vulnerabilities in Firmware
prompt_templates.prompt: What are the most common security vulnerabilities found in embedded firmware? Walk me through how to identify hardcoded credentials, command injection points, and insecure configurations in a extracted filesystem.
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate analyst focusing on vulnerability discovery

prompt_templates.title: Dynamic Analysis with Emulation
prompt_templates.prompt: How can I set up QEMU to emulate firmware binaries for dynamic analysis? I want to test network services and identify vulnerabilities without physical hardware.
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced analyst wanting to perform dynamic testing

output_examples.input: How do I analyze a firmware binary for the first time?
output: Start with Phase 1: Identification - Use 'file firmware.bin' to identify the file type, then run 'binwalk firmware.bin' to detect embedded filesystems and compressed data. Check entropy with 'binwalk --entropy firmware.bin' to identify encrypted or compressed sections. For Phase 2: Extraction, use 'binwalk -eM firmware.bin' for recursive extraction. Then explore the filesystem in Phase 3 to find configuration files, hardcoded credentials, and web interfaces. Finally in Phase 4, analyze binaries with Ghidra or IDA Pro for vulnerabilities.

output_examples.input: What tools should I use for firmware analysis?
output: Key tools by category: Extraction - binwalk v3, firmware-mod-kit, jefferson for JFFS2, ubi_reader for UBIFS. Analysis - Ghidra for multi-architecture disassembly, checksec for binary security, FACT for automated analysis. Emulation - QEMU for user-mode and full system emulation, Firmadyne for automated emulation. Hardware - Bus Pirate for UART access, JTAGulator for debug discovery, Flashrom for flash reading.

best_practices: Always verify you have authorization before analyzing firmware from devices you do not own
Document your analysis process thoroughly including tools used, findings, and methodology for credible security reports
Use entropy analysis early to identify encrypted or compressed regions that may require additional techniques
anti_patterns: Running extracted binaries directly on your host system without proper isolation or emulation
Assuming firmware is harmless - always treat it as potentially malicious and analyze in isolated environments
Skipping the ethical verification step - ensure your analysis falls within authorized security research boundaries

faq.question: Can this skill help me extract firmware from a specific device?
faq.answer: This skill provides guidance on extraction methods including vendor downloads, UART, JTAG, and flash dumping. The specific method depends on your target device. Always ensure you have authorization to extract firmware from any device.

faq.question: What programming architectures can this skill analyze?
faq.answer: The skill covers common embedded architectures including ARM, MIPS, x86, and PowerPC. Ghidra supports these and many more architectures for disassembly and reverse engineering.

faq.question: Do I need special hardware for firmware analysis?
faq.answer: Basic analysis can be done entirely with software using extracted firmware files. Hardware tools like UART adapters, JTAG debuggers, or logic analyzers are only needed for physical firmware extraction from devices.

faq.question: How do I analyze encrypted firmware?
faq.answer: Encrypted firmware typically shows high entropy throughout. Look for decrypted sections in memory during emulation, check for hardware security module interactions, or investigate if the device decrypts firmware at boot time.

faq.question: Can this skill help me set up firmware emulation?
faq.answer: Yes, the skill provides guidance on QEMU user-mode emulation with chroot and full system emulation using Firmadyne. Emulation allows dynamic analysis of firmware binaries without physical hardware.

faq.question: What ethical guidelines should I follow?
faq.answer: Only analyze firmware you own, have authorization to test, or is covered by bug bounty programs. Never use findings for unauthorized access, create malicious firmware, or engage in industrial espionage. Always follow responsible disclosure practices.
